heroOverview: Orchitis is inflammation of one or both testes, most commonly caused by a bacterial infection that spreads from the epididymis or, in younger men, by the mumps virus. Bacterial orchitis accounts for roughly 60–80% of cases in adults and almost always co-exists with epididymitis; isolated orchitis most often follows mumps, which causes testicular inflammation in 20–30% of post-pubertal males who contract the disease. The condition produces sudden-onset scrotal pain, swelling, and fever. Prompt diagnosis matters because testicular atrophy occurs in up to 50% of mumps-related cases and untreated bacterial orchitis can progress to abscess. Most bacterial cases resolve fully with antibiotics and supportive care within one to two weeks.

keyStats.prevalence: Orchitis affects approximately 1 in 1,000 men annually; bacterial epididymo-orchitis is one of the most common urological infections in men aged 14–35
keyStats.demographics: Sexually active men aged 14–35 are most affected by bacterial orchitis; mumps orchitis predominantly strikes unvaccinated post-pubertal males
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in sexually active men aged 19–35; mumps orchitis peaks in adolescent and young adult males
keyStats.globalCases: Mumps orchitis remains the single leading cause of viral orchitis worldwide in populations with incomplete MMR vaccine coverage

definition: Orchitis (ICD-10: N45.2) is acute or chronic inflammation of the testicular parenchyma. Bacterial orchitis almost always results from ascending infection via the vas deferens or lymphatics from an infected epididymis — a combined state termed epididymo-orchitis — and the causative organisms follow the patient's age and sexual behaviour. In sexually active men under 35, Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae predominate; in men over 35, coliforms such as Escherichia coli predominate, often in association with structural urinary pathology such as benign prostatic enlargement. Isolated viral orchitis from mumps (paramyxovirus) occurs days after parotitis and can affect both testes in 15–30% of mumps cases. Rarer causes include tuberculosis, brucellosis, and autoimmune processes. The testes respond to infection with oedema, vascular congestion, and influx of inflammatory cells. Testicular function — both spermatogenesis and testosterone production — may be transiently or permanently impaired depending on severity and promptness of treatment. Orchitis falls primarily under urology, with infectious disease input when systemic infection or atypical organisms are suspected.

typesClassification.description: Usually part of epididymo-orchitis; caused by STI pathogens in younger men and coliforms in older men; treated with antibiotics.
Follows mumps parotitis by 4–8 days; accounts for up to 30% of orchitis cases; no specific antiviral treatment, managed supportively.
Caused by tuberculosis, brucellosis, or sarcoidosis; slow-onset, firm testicular mass; requires prolonged antimicrobial or immunomodulatory therapy.
Rare; results from anti-sperm antibody production, sometimes after vasectomy or trauma; can cause subfertility without classic infection signs.

primarySymptoms: Sudden-onset pain in one or both testes, ranging from aching discomfort to severe tenderness that makes walking difficult.
Swelling and enlargement of the affected testis, often appearing within hours of symptom onset.
Warmth and redness overlying the scrotal skin on the affected side.
Fever, typically 38–40°C, reflecting systemic infection or significant local inflammation.
Discharge from the urethra (in cases caused by sexually transmitted organisms such as gonorrhea or chlamydia).
Dysuria or a burning sensation when urinating, pointing toward a concurrent urethral infection.
Nausea and vomiting, particularly when pain is severe or systemic illness is present.
Groin or lower abdominal pain that can radiate from the inflamed testis.
Generalised fatigue, headache, or muscle aches — especially in mumps orchitis, where systemic viral illness accompanies the local inflammation.
Parotid gland swelling (jaw swelling) appearing several days before testicular symptoms in mumps orchitis.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild, aching testicular discomfort that worsens over 24–48 hours.
A low-grade fever emerging alongside scrotal heaviness.
Mild urethral discharge or discomfort urinating that precedes testicular swelling.
Recent mumps-like illness with jaw swelling in an unvaccinated man.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe testicular pain of less than 6 hours' duration, which must be assumed to be testicular torsion until proven otherwise by Doppler ultrasound.
Progressive scrotal swelling with fluctuation suggesting abscess formation.
High fever (above 39°C) with rigors and inability to keep fluids down, indicating systemic sepsis.
Bluish discolouration or visible ischaemia of the testis.
Symptoms unresolved or worsening after 48–72 hours of antibiotic treatment.

causes.description: In sexually active men under 35, these STI pathogens ascend from a urethral infection along the vas deferens to the epididymis and then the testis. Chlamydia is the more frequent cause in high-income countries, while gonorrhea remains dominant in areas with high STI prevalence.

In men over 35 and in those with urinary tract pathology, coliforms from the gastrointestinal flora reach the urinary tract and ascend to cause infection. Structural problems such as prostatic enlargement or urethral stricture facilitate this route.

Mumps orchitis develops in 20–30% of post-pubertal males with mumps, typically 4–8 days after parotitis. The virus infects testicular cells directly via haematogenous spread, provoking inflammation and sometimes lasting ischaemia.

TB orchitis results from haematogenous seeding of the testis during systemic disease or from direct spread from the epididymis. It produces a firm, painless or mildly tender mass and must be distinguished from a tumour.

A cause of orchitis in men with occupational or dietary exposure to livestock or unpasteurised dairy. The clinical picture resembles bacterial epididymo-orchitis but the organism requires prolonged combination antibiotic therapy.

Cystoscopy, urethral catheter placement, or other procedures introduce bacteria into the lower urinary tract that can ascend to cause epididymo-orchitis, particularly in men with pre-existing prostatic or urethral pathology.

riskFactors.description: Multiple or new sexual partners and inconsistent condom use increase exposure to chlamydia and gonorrhea, the dominant bacterial causes of orchitis in young men.
Mumps vaccination rates below 95% in a population allow outbreaks that expose unvaccinated post-pubertal males to mumps orchitis risk; two doses of MMR are highly protective.

riskFactors.factor: Age over 35 with urinary tract abnormality
riskFactors.description: Prostatic enlargement, urethral stricture, or prior urological surgery creates urinary stasis and instrumentation pathways for coliform organisms.
Indwelling or intermittent catheters introduce bacteria and provide a surface for ascending infection; minimising catheter use and duration reduces the risk.
Scarring from prior infection can impair drainage and increase susceptibility to recurrent ascending bacterial infection in the same anatomical axis.
HIV infection, immunosuppressive medication, or haematological malignancy impairs local defences, allowing atypical or more severe infection; optimal immune management reduces this risk.
Travel to endemic areas or occupational contact with livestock and unpasteurised dairy products raises brucellosis risk, an under-recognised cause of orchitis.

affectedDemographics: Sexually active men aged 14–35 (bacterial orchitis from STI pathogens)
Men over 35 with urinary tract structural abnormalities (coliform orchitis)
Unvaccinated or incompletely vaccinated post-pubertal males during mumps outbreaks
Men who have sex with men (higher rates of gonorrhea and chlamydia)
Men in occupations or regions with brucellosis exposure
diagnosisOverview: Clinical history and physical examination establish the working diagnosis. The combination of scrotal pain, tenderness localised to the testis, fever, and urethral symptoms in a young sexually active man strongly suggests bacterial orchitis or epididymo-orchitis. Testicular torsion must be excluded urgently because it presents identically but requires emergency surgery — colour Doppler ultrasound is the key investigation, showing increased blood flow in orchitis and absent flow in torsion. Urethral swabs or first-catch urine nucleic acid amplification tests (NAATs) identify chlamydia and gonorrhea. Urine culture guides antibiotic choice in older men with coliform infection. Blood tests including full blood count and C-reactive protein confirm inflammatory activity; blood cultures are added when systemic sepsis is suspected. Scrotal ultrasound also excludes abscess and tumour. In atypical presentations — subacute onset, bilateral involvement, firm testicular mass, or travel history — cultures for TB and serology for brucellosis are performed. The main differential diagnoses are testicular torsion (emergency), epididymitis without testicular involvement, inguinal hernia, hydrocele, and testicular tumour.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes orchitis (increased blood flow, oedema) from testicular torsion (absent flow), and detects abscess or hydrocele.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the scrotum and a probe moved over the skin; the test is painless and takes 15–20 minutes; results are immediate.

diagnosticTests.test: Urethral swab or first-catch urine NAAT
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects chlamydia and gonorrhea nucleic acid in men with urethral symptoms or when an STI is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You provide the first 10–20 ml of urine passed on arrival at the clinic; results are typically available within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coliform organisms and determines antibiotic sensitivities, most useful in men over 35.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clean mid-stream urine sample is sent to the laboratory; results take 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the systemic inflammatory response and, in severe cases, identifies bacteraemia requiring broader antibiotic therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venepuncture; FBC and CRP results are available within hours, culture results within 48–72 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: STI screen (HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B serology)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Concurrent STI testing is recommended whenever chlamydia or gonorrhea is found, as co-infections are common.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn at the same sitting as other tests; results within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies fluid collections, abscess, or testicular necrosis requiring surgical intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same procedure as Doppler; the radiologist notes whether any fluid collection is present and whether the testicular architecture is preserved.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends on the likely causative organism, and empirical antibiotic therapy is started before culture results return in most cases. In men under 35 where an STI cause is probable, UK and US guidelines recommend dual therapy covering both chlamydia and gonorrhea: intramuscular ceftriaxone 500 mg as a single dose for gonorrhea combined with doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10–14 days for chlamydia, along with notification and treatment of sexual partners. Partner notification is mandatory when an STI is confirmed. In men over 35 where coliforms are more likely, oral fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin) for 14 days are recommended, adjusted once culture and sensitivities are available. Supportive measures — scrotal elevation with a jockstrap, analgesics (NSAIDs such as ibuprofen or naproxen), and relative rest — significantly reduce pain and oedema throughout the treatment course. Most cases of bacterial orchitis resolve within two weeks on appropriate antibiotics, but full return of testicular volume can take several additional weeks. Mumps orchitis has no specific antiviral treatment; management is entirely supportive with NSAIDs and scrotal support, and corticosteroids are sometimes used but evidence for benefit is limited. Patients who develop an abscess require surgical drainage or, in refractory cases, orchiectomy. Men with high fever, systemic sepsis, or inability to tolerate oral medication are admitted for intravenous antibiotics until clinically stable, then stepped down to oral therapy. Follow-up at four to six weeks is important to confirm microbiological and clinical resolution and to assess for post-inflammatory atrophy or subfertility.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone (single IM dose) + doxycycline
medicalTreatments.description: First-line dual therapy for presumed STI-related orchitis in men under 35. Ceftriaxone 500 mg covers gonorrhea; doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10–14 days covers chlamydia and provides additional Gram-negative coverage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates above 90% for chlamydial epididymo-orchitis when the full course is completed; clinical resolution of pain and swelling in most cases within 3–5 days.

medicalTreatments.name: Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for coliform orchitis in men over 35; ofloxacin 200 mg twice daily or ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 14 days; also covers chlamydia. Avoided as primary STI therapy because of rising gonorrhea fluoroquinolone resistance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical resolution in approximately 85–95% of coliform epididymo-orchitis; less reliable for gonorrhea in areas with >5% fluoroquinolone resistance.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory analgesics reduce scrotal pain and oedema. Used as adjunct throughout antibiotic treatment and as the primary treatment in mumps orchitis where no antibiotic benefit exists.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant pain reduction in most patients within 24–48 hours; no impact on microbiological cure but improves quality of life during treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous antibiotics (ceftriaxone IV, aminoglycosides)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for men with high fever, vomiting precluding oral treatment, signs of sepsis, or failure of oral therapy; transitions to oral treatment once the patient is stable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fever resolution within 48–72 hours in approximately 90% of hospitalised bacterial orchitis cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Scrotal elevation and supportive care
medicalTreatments.description: Elevating the scrotum with a jockstrap or rolled towel reduces oedema and pain by improving venous and lymphatic drainage; ice packs in the first 24–48 hours can further reduce swelling.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Subjective pain reduction reported in the majority of patients; no controlled-trial data, but universally recommended as an adjunct in clinical guidelines.

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage of testicular abscess
surgicalOptions.description: When ultrasound confirms a loculated abscess that does not resolve with antibiotics, surgical drainage under general or regional anaesthesia allows decompression and microbiological sampling.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of abscess in >90% of cases; testicular preservation is achieved in most patients when the procedure is performed before extensive necrosis.

surgicalOptions.name: Orchiectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the testis is reserved for severe cases where abscess, necrosis, or suspected malignancy cannot be otherwise managed. Also performed if torsion mimicking orchitis is discovered intra-operatively with non-viable testis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive resolution of the infected focus; contralateral testis preserves fertility and androgen production in the majority of patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Scrotal support and ice packs
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-pharmacological measures that reduce oedema and pain; essential adjuncts to antibiotic therapy but not substitutes for it.

alternativeTreatments.name: Adequate rest and hydration
alternativeTreatments.description: Bed rest during the acute phase reduces discomfort; adequate fluid intake supports the immune response and helps maintain renal clearance of antibiotics.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sexual partner notification and treatment
alternativeTreatments.description: For STI-related orchitis, notifying and treating sexual partners is part of the treatment pathway; it reduces re-infection risk and is supported by most national STI guidelines.

whySeeSpecialist: A urologist should be involved when testicular torsion cannot be clinically excluded (requiring emergency evaluation), when scrotal ultrasound reveals an abscess or tumour, when orchitis fails to respond to 72 hours of appropriate antibiotics, or when there is bilateral involvement with concern for fertility. Infectious disease specialists are consulted for TB orchitis, brucellosis, or unusual organisms. Post-treatment follow-up with a urologist is appropriate when testicular atrophy develops, as androgen deficiency or subfertility may require further assessment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a urologist with experience in scrotal emergencies and access to same-day or on-call scrotal Doppler ultrasound — the ability to differentiate torsion from orchitis immediately is critical. For STI-related orchitis, a genitourinary medicine (GUM) or sexual health clinic offers integrated STI testing, partner notification, and follow-up. For complicated or atypical orchitis, a urologist with access to interventional radiology for abscess drainage reduces the chance of unnecessary orchiectomy.
preventionStrategies: Ensure full MMR vaccination with two doses; this nearly eliminates mumps orchitis risk.
Use barrier contraception consistently to reduce transmission of chlamydia and gonorrhea.
Attend regular sexual health screening if you have multiple partners, enabling early treatment before ascending infection develops.
Treat urethritis and STIs promptly; early antibiotic therapy prevents progression to epididymo-orchitis.
Minimise unnecessary urinary catheterisation and ensure strict aseptic technique when catheters are required.
Manage underlying urological conditions such as prostatic enlargement that create urinary stasis.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full antibiotic course, even after pain subsides, to prevent relapse.
Abstain from sexual intercourse until the infection is fully treated and any sexual partners have been tested and treated.
Wear a scrotal support (athletic supporter or brief underwear) during recovery to reduce discomfort and oedema.
Avoid strenuous physical activity until pain and swelling have fully resolved.
If fertility is a concern after mumps orchitis, seek a semen analysis three to six months after recovery.

recommended: Adequate fluid intake to support antibiotic clearance and general immune function
Anti-inflammatory foods such as oily fish, leafy vegetables, and fruits during the acute phase
Probiotic-containing foods if taking antibiotics prone to gut side effects
avoid: Alcohol during antibiotic treatment, particularly with doxycycline (increases nausea) and fluoroquinolones
Heavy physical exertion or heat exposure that worsens scrotal oedema during the acute phase

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is recommended during the acute phase of orchitis. Scrotal support should be worn even during light activities once symptoms begin to settle. Strenuous exercise, cycling, or contact sports should be avoided until pain, swelling, and fever have completely resolved — typically one to two weeks for bacterial orchitis. Gradual return to normal activity is safe once the follow-up ultrasound confirms resolution if imaging was obtained.
dailyManagement: Take antibiotics at the prescribed times and complete the full course without stopping early.
Elevate the scrotum while lying down using a rolled towel or folded pillow.
Apply a wrapped ice pack to the scrotum for 15–20 minutes several times a day in the first 48 hours.
Monitor for worsening pain, rising fever, or new scrotal fluctuation that could signal abscess formation.
Return promptly for review if symptoms do not improve within 72 hours of starting antibiotics.
prognosis: The outlook for bacterial orchitis treated promptly with appropriate antibiotics is good; most men recover fully within two weeks. Testicular atrophy, resulting from ischaemia and inflammation, affects approximately 30–50% of testes following mumps orchitis and is less common after bacterial orchitis when treatment is early. Subfertility can result if both testes are affected or if significant atrophy occurs; however, unilateral orchitis rarely causes clinically significant infertility because the contralateral testis compensates. Androgen deficiency from bilateral testicular damage is uncommon but should be evaluated if symptoms of hypogonadism develop after bilateral involvement. Abscess formation occurs in about 3–8% of bacterial orchitis cases and generally resolves with drainage, with testicular preservation achievable in most patients. Early treatment is the strongest determinant of a good outcome.
recoveryTimeline: Pain and fever usually begin to improve within 2–3 days of starting antibiotics. Scrotal swelling takes longer to resolve — typically 1–3 weeks. Full resolution of tenderness and return to normal activity usually occurs within 2–4 weeks. Testicular volume, if reduced by inflammation, may take 2–3 months to normalise; persistent atrophy after this time is likely permanent. A follow-up appointment at 4–6 weeks is recommended to confirm resolution and check for late complications.
complications: Testicular atrophy — reduction in testicular size from ischaemia and inflammation; affects up to 50% of testes after mumps orchitis and 3–10% after bacterial orchitis.
Testicular abscess — a walled-off collection of pus requiring surgical drainage; occurs in approximately 3–8% of bacterial orchitis cases.
Subfertility or infertility — bilateral orchitis with significant atrophy can impair spermatogenesis; unilateral disease rarely causes clinical infertility.
Epididymal scarring and obstruction — can impair sperm transport from the affected testis even after infection resolves.
Androgen deficiency — rarely, bilateral testicular damage reduces testosterone production, causing hypogonadal symptoms such as low libido, fatigue, and reduced muscle mass.
Chronic scrotal pain — a minority of men develop persistent testicular discomfort after the acute infection resolves, thought to be related to inflammatory nerve sensitisation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epididymitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epididymitis is inflammation confined to the epididymis, producing tenderness specifically at the posterior-superior pole of the testis; in orchitis the entire testis is tender and swollen, though the two frequently coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gonorrhea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gonococcal urethritis causes dysuria and urethral discharge without necessarily involving the testis; orchitis implies that the infection has ascended to produce testicular inflammation, confirmed on examination and ultrasound.

confusedWithConditions.name: Syphilis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Syphilitic gumma of the testis produces a firm painless testicular mass rather than acute tender swelling; serological testing distinguishes it from bacterial orchitis.

relatedConditions.name: Epididymo-orchitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The combined infection of the epididymis and testis is the most common form; orchitis rarely occurs without concurrent epididymitis in bacterial cases.

relatedConditions.name: Epididymitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Isolated epididymitis frequently progresses to orchitis if untreated; the same pathogens and treatment pathways apply.

relatedConditions.name: Gonorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: Neisseria gonorrhoeae is a major cause of STI-related orchitis in sexually active men and necessitates dual antibiotic therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Benign prostatic hyperplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Prostatic enlargement creates urinary stasis that predisposes older men to coliform orchitis via the ascending urinary route.

faqs.question: What is orchitis?
faqs.answer: Orchitis is inflammation of one or both testes, usually caused by a bacterial infection (often chlamydia or gonorrhea in younger men) or by the mumps virus. It produces sudden scrotal pain, swelling, and fever.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have orchitis or testicular torsion?
faqs.answer: Both cause sudden severe testicular pain, but torsion is a surgical emergency requiring treatment within 6 hours. The only reliable way to distinguish them is a colour Doppler ultrasound that shows blood flow in orchitis and absent flow in torsion. Any sudden severe testicular pain needs emergency assessment — do not wait.

faqs.question: Is orchitis caused by an STI?
faqs.answer: In sexually active men under 35, orchitis is most often caused by chlamydia or gonorrhea spreading from the urethra. In older men, gut bacteria (coliforms) are more common. Mumps virus causes orchitis in unvaccinated post-pubertal males. Not all orchitis is sexually transmitted.

faqs.question: How is orchitis treated?
faqs.answer: Bacterial orchitis is treated with antibiotics — dual therapy (ceftriaxone plus doxycycline) for STI causes, or fluoroquinolones for coliform causes. Pain is managed with NSAIDs and scrotal elevation. Mumps orchitis has no specific antiviral treatment; supportive care controls symptoms.

faqs.question: Can orchitis affect fertility?
faqs.answer: Orchitis can impair fertility if both testes are significantly inflamed or if atrophy develops. Mumps orchitis causes atrophy in up to 50% of affected testes. Unilateral bacterial orchitis treated promptly rarely causes clinically significant infertility because the other testis compensates.

faqs.question: How long does orchitis take to heal?
faqs.answer: Pain and fever typically improve within 2–3 days of starting antibiotics. Swelling takes 1–3 weeks to resolve. Full recovery and return to normal activity usually occur within 2–4 weeks. Follow-up at 4–6 weeks confirms resolution.

faqs.question: Does the MMR vaccine prevent orchitis?
faqs.answer: Two doses of the MMR vaccine provide strong protection against mumps, eliminating the risk of mumps orchitis. The MMR vaccine does not prevent bacterial orchitis caused by STI pathogens or coliforms.

faqs.question: What happens if orchitis is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated bacterial orchitis can progress to abscess formation (in about 3–8% of cases), testicular necrosis, and persistent atrophy. Mumps orchitis without management also risks atrophy. Early treatment is important to preserve testicular function.

faqs.question: Can I have sex while being treated for orchitis?
faqs.answer: You should abstain from sexual intercourse until the full antibiotic course is completed and symptoms have resolved. If an STI was the cause, your sexual partners must be tested and treated before you resume sexual contact to prevent re-infection.

faqs.question: Will orchitis cause testicular shrinkage?
faqs.answer: Testicular atrophy occurs in up to 50% of mumps orchitis cases and in a smaller proportion of bacterial orchitis cases — most commonly when treatment is delayed or incomplete. Atrophy that persists beyond 2–3 months is likely permanent.

faqs.question: Is orchitis the same as epididymitis?
faqs.answer: No, but they frequently occur together. Epididymitis is inflammation of the epididymis (the coiled tube behind the testis), while orchitis involves the testis itself. When both are inflamed it is called epididymo-orchitis, the most common form of bacterial scrotal infection.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for orchitis?
faqs.answer: For STI-related orchitis in men under 35, ceftriaxone 500 mg IM plus doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10–14 days is recommended. For coliform orchitis in men over 35, ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin for 14 days is standard. The choice depends on age, sexual history, and culture results.

faqs.question: Can orchitis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Orchitis can recur if an untreated STI cause is not fully cleared, if a sexual partner is not treated, or if an underlying urological abnormality remains. Ensuring partners are treated, completing the full antibiotic course, and treating any contributing structural pathology reduces recurrence risk.

faqs.question: Should I go to the emergency room for orchitis?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you have sudden severe testicular pain — this requires emergency Doppler ultrasound to exclude testicular torsion, which is a surgical emergency. You should also seek urgent care for high fever with rigors, rapidly worsening scrotal swelling, or symptoms not improving after 48–72 hours of antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can orchitis affect testosterone levels?
faqs.answer: Bilateral orchitis with significant atrophy can reduce testosterone production, causing symptoms such as low libido, fatigue, reduced muscle mass, and mood changes. This is uncommon after unilateral orchitis. A blood test measuring testosterone and gonadotrophins can diagnose this if symptoms develop.

faqs.question: Is orchitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Orchitis itself is not contagious. However, the underlying infection causing it may be. Mumps is highly contagious via respiratory droplets. STI-related orchitis means the causative bacteria (chlamydia or gonorrhea) can be passed to sexual partners, which is why partner notification is essential.

faqs.question: What is mumps orchitis?
faqs.answer: Mumps orchitis is testicular inflammation caused by the mumps virus, occurring in 20–30% of post-pubertal males who contract mumps. It typically begins 4–8 days after jaw swelling (parotitis), affects one testis in most cases, and has no specific antiviral treatment. Two MMR vaccine doses prevent it.

faqs.question: How is orchitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines clinical examination with colour Doppler scrotal ultrasound (to confirm blood flow and exclude torsion), urethral swab or urine NAAT (for chlamydia and gonorrhea), urine culture (for coliforms), and blood tests. STI serology is often added.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for orchitis?
faqs.answer: Most cases of orchitis resolve with antibiotics and supportive care without surgery. Surgery (drainage or, rarely, orchiectomy) is needed only if an abscess forms or if the testis becomes non-viable. Torsion initially mimicking orchitis may also require emergency surgery.

faqs.question: Can orchitis cause long-term pain?
faqs.answer: A minority of men develop chronic scrotal pain after orchitis resolves. This persists beyond 3 months and is thought to involve inflammatory nerve sensitisation. Chronic scrotal pain after orchitis warrants urological review to exclude undertreated infection, epididymal cyst, or varicocele.

metaDescription: Orchitis causes scrotal pain, swelling and fever from bacterial STIs or mumps. Learn symptoms, antibiotic treatment and fertility effects. Doctor-reviewed.
